topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating persistence. Liam stared at the screen, his jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. The spreadsheet was undeniable. Sarah, the junior analyst who usually shuffled papers with a nervous smile, had just clocked in a salary that dwarfed his own. A slow, hot pressure began to build behind Liam's eyes. He clenched his fist on the desk, the cheap laminate groaning under the sudden tension. He felt a strange, cold clarity settle over him, replacing the usual dull throb of workplace annoyance. When Sarah finally walked by, humming a cheerful tune, Liam didn't look up. He just watched her, his gaze sharp and unblinking, a silent promise of future reckoning hanging heavy in the air between them.

[story 2]

"Are you quite alright, Mark?" Brenda asked, noticing the sudden rigidity in his posture. Mark felt a violent surge of heat rise from his chest, making his palms slick. He’d just discovered the disparity in compensation figures. It wasn't just a small difference; it was a chasm. His breath hitched, shallow and rapid. He stood up abruptly, knocking his chair slightly askew. His voice, when he finally spoke, was dangerously level, stripped bare of its usual corporate pleasantries. "I need to speak with HR immediately," he managed, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the conference table. Every muscle in his body felt coiled, ready to spring.

[story 3]

The silence in the breakroom was deafening after the revelation. David leaned against the counter, his hands balling into fists deep in his pockets. The numbers had hit him like a physical blow. A tremor ran through his hands, making the lukewarm coffee cup tremble precariously in his grasp. He looked around the room, his eyes sweeping over the oblivious colleagues, a simmering intensity radiating off him. He took a deep, shaky breath, forcing the air into his lungs, trying to keep the shaking contained. When he finally spoke, his tone was low, almost a hiss, laced with an unmistakable edge of controlled fury. "This isn't right," he stated, the words vibrating with suppressed force.
